---
title: "The Future of Information Control"
subtitle: "Internet Censorship and Online Speech"
author: "Nick Feamster · University of Chicago"
header-includes: |
  <style>
    /* No ALL-CAPS anywhere */
    .reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3, .reveal h4, .reveal h5, .reveal h6,
    .reveal .title, .reveal .subtitle,
    section.title-slide h1, section.title-slide .subtitle {
      text-transform: none !important;
      letter-spacing: normal !important;
    }
    /* Uniform title sizes (absolute px — unaffected by .smaller) */
    .reveal h1, .reveal .smaller h1, .reveal section.smaller h1 { font-size: 46px !important; }
    .reveal h2, .reveal .smaller h2, .reveal section.smaller h2 { font-size: 38px !important; }
    .reveal h3, .reveal .smaller h3, .reveal section.smaller h3 { font-size: 30px !important; }
    section.title-slide h1 { font-size: 56px !important; }
    section.title-slide .subtitle { font-size: 32px !important; }
    /* Body sizing (a bit bigger than before) */
    .reveal { font-size: 30px; }
    .reveal .smaller, .reveal section.smaller { font-size: 0.78em; }
    .reveal ul, .reveal ol, .reveal p, .reveal blockquote { font-size: 0.88em; }
    .reveal li { line-height: 1.3; margin-bottom: 0.15em; }
    .reveal table { font-size: 0.72em; }
    .reveal pre, .reveal code { font-size: 0.72em; }
  </style>
---

## From Walls to Currents {.center}

> "The Net interprets censorship as damage and routes around it."
> — John Gilmore, *Time*, December 6, 1993

Twenty years ago the problem looked simple: the censor built a **wall**, we
routed around it. This last lecture asks what replaced that world — and who
decides what comes next.

::: {.notes}
Bookend the course with the same quote we opened on (Ch. 1). The book's
conclusion frames the shift: we have moved "from a world of walls to a world of
currents" — information now flows through channels shaped by algorithms,
platform policy, economics, and law, not neutral pipes. The firewall didn't
disappear (the GFW keeps evolving) — it was joined by subtler, more pervasive,
harder-to-counter techniques. See censorship-book Ch. 7.
:::

# What We Learned {.center}

## Information Control, Not Just "Censorship"

The course's central reframing: control is any **tax on access to information** —
a ban, but also **friction** (slow, bury) and **flooding** (drown out).

- **Fear · Friction · Flooding** — Roberts's taxonomy, our through-line
- Modern control leans on the quiet, deniable end: friction and flooding
- A **sociotechnical** problem — code *and* law *and* economics *and* norms

::: {.notes}
Restate the thesis one last time. Most modern control isn't a firewall that says
BLOCKED — it's a toll most people won't pay. Keep the "tax on access" vocabulary
from Ch. 1 consistent through the wrap-up.
:::

## Five Chapters, Five Levers {.smaller}

| Lever | What we saw | Book |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical** | DNS manipulation, BGP hijack, DPI, throttling that hides as congestion | Ch. 2 |
| **Platform** | moderation at impossible scale, the 50 Cent Party, AI arbiters | Ch. 3 |
| **Legal / economic** | copyright takedowns, net neutrality, zero-rating, demonetization, paywalls | Ch. 4 |
| **Measurement** | turning invisible control into evidence — OONI, Censored Planet | Ch. 5 |
| **Circumvention** | VPNs, Tor, domain fronting, decoy routing — and their limits | Ch. 6 |

::: {.notes}
This is the whole course on one slide — the book's chapter arc. Each row is a
deck. The key insight from Ch. 3: censorship need not block anything — flooding
and strategic amplification shape discourse just as well. From Ch. 4: economic
and legal controls operate below public attention but rival any firewall.
:::

## Measurement Is Resistance

Control is often **designed to look like ordinary failure** — a slow load, a
timeout. Measurement is how we tell censorship from a bad day on the network.

- Without it we cannot know the **extent**, hold actors **accountable**, or
  evaluate **countermeasures**
- But it carries its own ethics: **researcher and volunteer safety**, and tools
  that can be **repurposed for surveillance**

::: {.notes}
Ch. 5's takeaway, stated as a principle students should keep. The measurement
field exists precisely because modern control is deniable.
:::

# The Paradox of the Open Internet {.center}

## The Same Openness, Turned Against Itself

At the **protocol layer**, Gilmore's dictum still mostly holds — BGP routes
around failures, TCP retransmits, DNS resolves.

**Above** the protocol layer, consolidation concentrated power the designers
never anticipated:

- A handful of **platforms** mediate most online activity
- A handful of **CDNs** (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly) are choke points
- A handful of **cloud providers** host nearly everything

::: {.notes}
The book's core paradox: the openness that let these platforms grow also lets
them exercise unprecedented control. When AWS terminates a customer, Cloudflare
stops proxying, or Google pulls an app, the effect can be as complete as any
government firewall — and without constitutional constraints, due process, or
transparency obligations.
:::

## Private Power, No Constitution {.smaller}

When **Amazon** drops a customer, **Cloudflare** stops proxying a site, or
**Google** removes an app — the effect can be as total as any state firewall.

But these are **private companies in competitive markets**. Their decisions face:

- No First Amendment constraint
- No due-process requirement
- No transparency obligation

The Internet's freedom was always **more aspiration than guarantee** —
consolidation revealed how fragile that aspiration was.

::: {.notes}
This is the punchline of the paradox. The point isn't that platforms are evil —
it's that we built our public square on infrastructure governed by contract law,
not constitutional law. Connects directly to the governance question coming up.
:::

# The Impossible Tradeoffs {.center}

## No Neutral Position {.smaller}

Content moderation at the scale of **billions of users across dozens of legal
regimes** admits no neutral move:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Remove** content → censorship
- **Don't remove** → a distribution channel
- **Inconsistent** rules → arbitrary
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Consistent** rules at scale → errors guaranteed
- **Human** moderators → trauma
- **Automated** systems → opaque, unappealable
:::
:::

Defer to local law → **complicit in censorship**. Impose one global standard →
**cultural imperialism**.

::: {.notes}
Walk one or two of these pairs out loud. The point from the book: there is no
position outside the tradeoff. Every choice prioritizes some values over others.
:::

## Tradeoffs All the Way Down

- **Economic** — **paywalls** fund quality journalism *but* sort readers into
  the **informed-rich and the misinformation-poor** (free content skews to the
  sensational); zero-rating expands access *but* distorts competition;
  demonetization protects advertisers *but* incentivizes self-censorship
- **Legal** — §230 enabled user content *but* limited accountability; the EU
  **DSA** adds due process *but* its fines may encourage **over-removal**;
  copyright protects creators *but* arms takedown abuse

These are **not problems to solve but tensions to manage**. The real question:
*who* decides, through *what* process, with *what* accountability?

::: {.notes}
Book §"The Impossible Tradeoffs." Land the framing: not "what's the right
answer" but "who gets to choose, and are they accountable." On paywalls, push the
information-inequality point harder than the book's one line: a "tax on access"
in the Ch. 1 sense — reliable reporting goes behind a paywall while free,
ad-driven, outrage-optimized content stays open, so the people least able to pay
get the least reliable information (see the Ch. 4 / Legal-Economic deck). That
pivots us into emerging challenges and governance.
:::

# Emerging Challenges {.center}

## Generative AI Breaks the Epistemics

When **any** text, image, or voice might be synthetic, the foundations of public
discourse erode.

- The challenge is no longer just removing harmful content — it's establishing
  **any basis for distinguishing truth from fabrication**
- AI is also the **arbiter**: ranking search, personalizing feeds, flagging
  content — at scales that make human review impossible
- When an AI system suppresses a result, **no human may be able to explain why**

::: {.vignette}
**Provenance is a partial answer, not a fix.** **C2PA Content Credentials** —
cryptographically signed manifests you can check in-browser at
`verify.contentauthenticity.org` — now ship natively from the **Pixel 10** and
**Galaxy S25**, and OpenAI is layering C2PA with SynthID (2026). The **EU AI Act
Art. 50** machine-readable-disclosure rules begin enforcement **Aug 2026**. But
provenance proves *who signed and that the bits are unaltered* — **not that the
content is true**, and absent credentials prove nothing.
:::

::: {.notes}
The book calls generative AI the most significant shift since social media — a
double hit: it floods the zone with synthetic content *and* it adjudicates
access opaquely. Tie back to Ch. 3's "AI arbiter." The C2PA add answers the
obvious student question ("can't we just watermark it?"): provenance/watermarking
is a real, deploying countermeasure, but it authenticates the manifest, not the
claim, and unsigned content is the common case. Sources: contentauthenticity.org
"State of Content Authenticity 2026"; EU AI Act Art. 50 (enforcement Aug 2026).
This will date fast — swap adoption/enforcement specifics each year.
:::

## The Encrypted-DNS / ECH Arms Race {.smaller}

Encryption keeps moving the censor's target. **Encrypted Client Hello (ECH)**
hides the SNI that censors filter on — so the fight moved **down the stack**.

::: {.vignette}
**China and Iran don't break ECH — they block the encrypted-DNS lookups that
bootstrap it.** Per a FOCI 2025 study (PETS, July 2025), the Great Firewall
censors the DNS needed to fetch ECH keys rather than the ECH payload itself; the
authors circumvented it in all three of China, Iran, and Russia. On **May 23,
2025**, `quic-go` v0.52.0 shipped **SNI-slicing** to defeat the GFW's QUIC
SNI-based censorship — the cat-and-mouse continues.
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the freshest technical hook and the cleanest illustration of the
"no stable equilibrium" point from Ch. 6/7. Teaching point: the censor attacks
the weakest link in the chain (here, DNS bootstrap), not the crypto. Sources:
gfw.report USENIX Security 25; FOCI 2025 paper foci-2025-0016. Swap forward each
year as the GFW/ECH state moves.
:::

## Encryption Itself Is Contested

The same arms race plays out in **law**, not just protocols:

::: {.vignette}
The EU's **"Chat Control"** (CSA Regulation) sought to mandate client-side
scanning of messages. After a German-led blocking minority, the Danish
presidency **dropped mandatory scanning** (Council political agreement, Nov 26,
2025); in **April 2026** the European Parliament also blocked extending
voluntary mass-scanning. Mandatory **age verification** and trilogue fights
remain. *(EFF, Apr 2026.)*
:::

Cryptographers: **backdoors break security for everyone.** Governments: **we
need access.** Platforms: caught in the middle.

::: {.notes}
The encryption-governance dilemma from the merged governance source. Note the UK
Investigatory Powers Act and Australia's Assistance & Access Act as parallels.
The win here is real but fragile — flag it as a refresh point.
:::

## Geopolitics Fragments the Network {.smaller}

The dream of one global Internet is giving way to **nationally-controlled
networks** — the **"splinternet."**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Evidence for**

- National firewalls (China, Russia, Iran)
- Data-localization laws in 60+ countries
- Divergent regional rules (DSA, India's IT Rules)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Evidence against**

- TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS still globally unified
- Cross-border data still flows
- Platforms **adapt** rather than split
:::
:::

Reality: **technical unity, regulatory fragmentation.**

::: {.notes}
Russia's "sovereign internet" (RuNet, 2019 law; DPI mandates; disconnection
tests) and China's Great Firewall + Digital Silk Road are the sharp cases — they
aren't just blocking, they're exporting an alternative governance *model*. The EU
is the "third way" (GDPR, DSA, DMA, AI Act) and the Brussels Effect.
:::

## Data Localization Has an Economics {.smaller}

The splinternet isn't only firewalls — it's **where bytes are allowed to rest.**
"**Cloud sovereignty**" turns data location into law, market structure, and a
**tax on access.**

- Localization mandates force in-country data centers → **higher cost**, fewer
  providers, advantage to incumbents that can afford to comply
- Cross-border transfer turns on **legal frameworks**, not cables

::: {.vignette}
The **EU–U.S. Data Privacy Framework** — the legal basis for moving Europeans'
data to U.S. clouds — **survived its first court challenge** (EU General Court,
*Latombe*, **Sept 3, 2025**), but is **on appeal to the CJEU** (C-703/25 P, no
hearing set as of mid-2026). A "**Schrems III**" strike-down would again strand
transatlantic data flows — exactly the uncertainty driving EU "**sovereign
cloud**" demand. *(IAPP; Freshfields, 2025–26.)*
:::

::: {.notes}
This promotes the one-clause "data-localization laws in 60+ countries" from the
splinternet slide into its own teaching point: regulatory fragmentation has a
*price*, and that price is itself a form of information control — a tax on
access in the Ch. 1 sense. The DPF/Schrems saga is the live example: Privacy
Shield (Schrems II, 2020) was struck down, the DPF replaced it (2023), and its
durability is again before the courts. Note the FISA §702 / PCLOB-independence
worries underneath the appeal. Refresh: track C-703/25 P and any noyb "Schrems
III" filing. Sources: IAPP and Freshfields coverage of *Latombe* (Sept 2025).
:::

## Even the Shutdown — the Bluntest Tool — Is Rising {.smaller}

::: {.vignette}
Access Now's **KeepItOn** coalition counted **313 internet shutdowns across 52
countries in 2025** — its highest annual count (up from **296** in 2024).
**Conflict** was the leading trigger for the third straight year, and **70
shutdowns in 21 countries** coincided with grave human-rights abuses.
*(Report released March 31, 2026.)*
:::

Even as control gets subtle, the crudest instrument is at an **all-time high**.

::: {.notes}
The companion data point to the technical-subtlety story: friction and flooding
rise, *and* full shutdowns hit records — concentrated in conflict and elections.
Swap this number each year (see coverage-notes). internetshutdowns.in and OONI
track this live.
:::

# Who Decides? Internet Governance {.center}

## Governance Was Never Absent

> "The development and application by governments, the private sector and civil
> society... of shared principles, norms, rules, decision-making procedures, and
> programmes that shape the evolution and use of the Internet."
> — World Summit on the Information Society, 2005

The Internet is **not ungoverned — it never was.** The question is *who*, *how*,
*for whom*, and *with what legitimacy*.

::: {.notes}
Counter John Perry Barlow's 1996 "Declaration of the Independence of Cyberspace."
The multi-stakeholder model: governments, private sector, civil society,
technical community — no single absolute authority, but unequal power in
practice.
:::

## The Multi-Stakeholder Model and Its Challengers {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Multi-stakeholder** (today's default)

- **ICANN** — names, numbers, the root zone
- **IETF** — protocols and standards
- **RIRs** — IP allocation
- Plus platforms, civil society
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Multilateral** (Russia, China push)

- **ITU** / UN-style state voting
- **"Cyber sovereignty"** — each nation controls its space
- Government-to-government control
:::
:::

A control-vs-openness divide playing out in every standards body.

::: {.notes}
The ICANN .io case is a nice concrete: a "technical" country-code decision (UK
returning the Chagos / BIOT to Mauritius, 2024) becomes geopolitical and
economic. Technical decisions are political decisions.
:::

## New Models for *Platform* Governance {.smaller}

Governance is also being re-invented **above** the infrastructure layer — three
live experiments in *who decides* what stays up:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Meta Oversight Board** — an external "court" Meta funds but doesn't control;
  **~200 decisions, ~320 recommendations** in 5 years (Dec 2025), expanding to
  **account-level** review in 2026
- **Bluesky** — **composable moderation**: independent **labelers** tag content;
  *clients/users* choose which to apply
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Mastodon** — **federation**: thousands of independently-run servers, each
  with its own rules and block-lists; no central authority
- The trade: **legitimacy / user choice** vs. **inconsistency** and weak
  defenses against well-resourced abuse
:::
:::

Each shifts the lever — **outsource it, unbundle it, or distribute it** — but
none escapes the impossible tradeoffs.

::: {.notes}
The concrete "emerging governance models" the conclusion gestures at. Map each to
the earlier tradeoff slide: the Oversight Board *outsources* the decision to a
quasi-judicial body (still Meta-funded — Meta committed funding through 2028,
reported June 2026); Bluesky *unbundles* it so moderation is a marketplace of
labelers rather than one network-wide ban; Mastodon *distributes* it across
federated instances. None is a neutral position — they relocate the tradeoff, not
dissolve it. Sources: Oversight Board H2-2025 / 5-year report; Bluesky
"Moderation Architecture" docs; MediaPost (funding through 2028, June 2026).
:::

## Jurisdiction: Global Network, National Laws {.smaller}

- **Right to be Forgotten** (Google Spain, 2014) — EU link removal; but the 2019
  ruling said **no global** application
- **Australia v. X/Meta** (2024) — a national takedown order demanding **global**
  removal of the Sydney church-stabbing video; the court later lifted it
- **India's IT Rules (2021)** — 36-hour takedowns, traceability that threatens
  end-to-end encryption, local grievance officers

If one country's law applies globally → **race to the bottom**. If not →
enforcement fragments.

::: {.notes}
This is the cross-border enforcement bind. There's no clean answer — most
restrictive jurisdiction wins, or enforcement becomes geo-fenced and messy.
Keep it short; the point is the structural tension, not the case details.
:::

# Paths Forward {.center}

## Directions, Not Solutions {.smaller}

The book offers **directions**, not a fix:

1. **Transparency** — reports, vetted research access, algorithmic explainability
2. **Technical countermeasures** — circumvention, encryption, decentralization
   (necessary, not sufficient)
3. **Smarter regulation** — due process for removals, interoperability mandates,
   antitrust against consolidation
4. **Digital literacy** — a core civic competency, not just individual duty
5. **Public deliberation** — the values our information environment encodes are
   choices about the society we want

::: {.notes}
Book §"Paths Forward." Stress #2's caveat: no technical solution addresses
consolidation, commercialization, or geopoliticization. And #5 is the throughline
into the final reflection — these are public choices, not engineering defaults.
:::

## The Problem Is Primarily Human {.center}

The author began thinking censorship was a **technical** problem. He came to see
it as a **human** one.

> The information environment is not a natural phenomenon but a **constructed**
> one — shaped by decisions of individuals, organizations, and governments.

Sobering: **no purely technical fix.** Empowering: **different choices can create
different outcomes.**

::: {.notes}
The book's "Final Reflection." This is the emotional center of the wrap-up. The
information environment was built by choices, so it can be rebuilt by choices.
:::

## What You Carry Out of This Course {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
You now know:

- **How** control works (technically)
- **Why** it happens (motives)
- **Who** does it (actors)
- **How** to measure it
- **How** to route around it
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
Carry the harder questions:

- Who *should* decide?
- Is the splinternet inevitable? Desirable?
- Can platforms be accountable **without** state control?
- How do we govern tech that doesn't exist yet?
:::
:::

These are **tensions to manage, not problems to solve.**

::: {.notes}
The discussion-question slide, distilled. Use as the cold-call / open-floor close.
:::

# The Struggle Continues {.center}

The Internet has always been **contested terrain** — control vs. freedom,
commerce vs. public value, sovereignty vs. connectivity.

Whether the future looks like the **open network** the pioneers imagined or the
**controlled environments** authoritarians prefer is **not predetermined.**
What happens next is up to all of us.

*See censorship-book Ch. 7 Conclusion: The Future of Information Control (whole chapter).*

::: {.notes}
Final slide. End on the book's last line — the future is a choice made by
technologists, policymakers, executives, and citizens. Thank the class; point to
final-project deadlines on the schedule. Stay curious, critical, engaged.
:::
